entry.title: 🇲🇽 ABAP AI: Capabilities in SAP Joule for Developers CodeJam (Monterrey, Mexico)
entry.content:
In this CodeJam session, you will learn how to use ABAP capabilities in SAP Joule for Developers, including predictive code completion, unit test generation for ABAP CDS and ABAP code, and code explanation to speed up your development with ABAP Cloud.

Join us for this day event of developer networking and hands-on learning at the SAP Labs Latin America, Avenida Ricardo Margain Zozaya, Zona Santa Engracia, San Pedro Garza García, Nuevo Leon, Mexico.

As prerequisites for the event, you need to install ADT in your local machine, you can see the instructions here: https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/abap-install-adt.html.

This free, hands-on, in-person event (not virtual) will take place on Thursday, May 25th 2026, in Monterrey, Mexico at the SAP Labs Latin America offices.

The instructors for this event are the Developer Advocates @thomas_jung and @RichHeilman .

Please note that spaces are limited; we can only accommodate 30 attendees. If you wish to attend, please indicate that using the "Yes" button in the Will you be attending? box on this page. If you decide not to come, please make sure to unregister using the "No" button in the Will you be attending? box to open up your seat for others.

entry.title: 🇲🇽 Build code-based AI Agents on SAP Business Technology Platform (Mexico City, Mexico)
entry.content:
We hit the limit of 30 attendees on April 2nd and all RSVPs after that will be considered a waiting list.

In this CodeJam, you will learn how to build state-of-the-art AI agents using Generative AI Hub, Python and JavaScript. You will also gain the skills to create custom tools for your agents, including leveraging SAP's RPT-1 model. Finally, you will deploy your agents to BTP.

In this CodeJam you can choose to build your agents with Python, and CrewAI or with JavaScript and LangGraph.

The CodeJam will include the following SAP technologies:
- Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core
- SAP RPT-1
- Grounding Service
- SAP AI Launchpad
- Business Application Studio (Python, JavaScript)

Exercise Content: https://github.com/SAP-samples/codejam-code-based-agents

Prerequisites: Chromium-based browser

This is a free in-person event and is planned for Tuesday, May 26, in Mexico City, Mexico 🇲🇽 from 09:30 to 15:30 PM local time. The event's language and content will be in English. 

The event is at the IBM Mexico offices -Alfonso Napoles Gandara 3111, Santa Fe, Zedec Santa Fé, Mexico City, CDMX, Mexico. A huge thank you to IBM for hosting this event.

The instructors are Developer Advocates @RichHeilman and @thomas_jung.

Please note that space is limited, as we can only accommodate 30 attendees.

entry.title: 🇲🇽 Build code-based AI Agents on SAP Business Technology Platform (Monterrey, Mexico)
entry.content:
In this CodeJam, you will learn how to build state-of-the-art AI agents using Generative AI Hub, Python and JavaScript. You will also gain the skills to create custom tools for your agents, including leveraging SAP's RPT-1 model. Finally, you will deploy your agents to BTP.

In this CodeJam you can choose to build your agents with Python, and CrewAI or with JavaScript and LangGraph.

The CodeJam will include the following SAP technologies:
- Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core
- SAP RPT-1
- Grounding Service
- SAP AI Launchpad
- Business Application Studio (Python, JavaScript)

Exercise Content: https://github.com/SAP-samples/codejam-code-based-agents

Prerequisites: Chromium-based browser

This is a free in-person event and is planned for Friday, May 29, in Monterrey, Mexico 🇲🇽 from 09:30 to 15:30 PM local time. The event's language and content will be in English. The event location is Paseo del Acueducto 2611, Del Paseo, 64920 Monterrey. Thanks to Tecmilenio for hosting.

Please note that space is limited, as we can only accommodate 30 attendees.

entry.title: Meetup - Pre-SIT Madrid 2026
entry.content:
¡Hola comunidad SAP en Madrid! ¿Qué les parece si nos juntamos la noche antes del SAP Inside Track Madrid 2026? La idea es reunirnos para hacer networking, compartir lo vivido en el CodeJam del mismo día (para los que asistieron) y ponernos en modo "evento" para el SIT del día siguiente. Comida y bebida a cargo de cada uno.

Tenemos una reserva para 15 personas en Restaurante Cazorla, Calle de Juan de Mena, Madrid, Spain. Por favor confirmar la asistencia con la funcionalidad de RSVP de esta pagina.

------

Hola SAP Community in Madrid! Join us the evening before SAP Inside Track Madrid 2026 for an informal dinner and networking session. Great chance to meet fellow SIT attendees, swap stories from the CodeJam earlier that day (for those who attend the CodeJam), and kick off the event week in style. Each person covers their own food and drinks.

We have a reservation for 15 at Restaurante Cazorla, Calle de Juan de Mena, Madrid, Spain. Please confirm that you will be attending by using the RSVP functionality on this web page.

entry.title: 🇵🇹 Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core (Lisboa, Portugal)
entry.content:
This SAP CodeJam event is on the topic of Getting started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core and Python. You do not need to have any background in the topic, but a lot of curiosity!

In this CodeJam you will learn how to use Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core to implement a retrieval augmented generation (RAG) use case to improve the responses of large language models (LLMs) and reduce hallucinations. You will learn how to deploy an LLM and the orchestration service on SAP AI Core and query it via SAP AI Launchpad and the Python SDK. Furthermore, you will learn about the most important genAI concepts and create and use embeddings to improve your RAG response and build your own chatbot with memory.

Prerequisites: Bring your own laptop with power adapter and have a supported Web browser such as Google Chrome or Firefox installed on your laptop.

This is an in-person event only (not virtual) and is planned for Wednesday 24th June 2026 in Lisboa, Portugal from 09:30 - 15:30 local time. Thanks to Nagarro for hosting. 

The language and content of the event will be in English.

Location: Nagarro, Rua Capitão Leitão, 21, 1950-050 Lisboa

Please note that spaces are limited - we can only accommodate 20 attendees. If you wish to attend, register by clicking the Yes button in the Will you be attending? section above to the right.

The instructor for this event is Developer Advocate @ajmaradiaga

entry.title: Re: AI Developer Challenge - Week 1: Grounding with SAP Generative AI Hub
entry.content: Completed week 1 - User GE305295

entry.title: Micro-Integrations: Redefining How You Design and Deploy Event-Driven Solutions
entry.content:
Apologies, we need to move the event to the 13th of May...

Event-driven integration is evolving, and micro-integrations are leading the way.

In this episode, we explore the micro-integrations architectural pattern: what it is, why it matters, and how it changes the way you design and deploy integrations in modern, cloud-native environments.

What you'll learn:

  • What micro-integrations are and how they differ from traditional integration approaches
  • Core design principles:  lightweight, independently deployable, event-driven components that do one thing well
  • Real-world use cases: how SAP and Solace customers are using micro-integrations to connect systems, transform data, and react to business events in real time

Target Audience: Developers, solution architects, and integration specialists working with SAP environments who want to modernize their integration strategy with event-driven patterns.

entry.title: Re: SAP Integration Suite API Management Meets Agentic AI
entry.content: Where can I find this MCP server that you are referring to?

entry.title: May 2026 Developer Challenge - From events to intelligence: Building an event-driven RAG pipeline
entry.content:
This month's developer challenge is a bit different from previous ones. Instead of focusing exclusively on SAP technologies, the goal is for you to learn the concepts, architect a solution and implement it using whatever tools you have access to — SAP, open-source, trial, paid products, use your favourite programming language or whatever agent you fancy using. At the end of each week's challenge, I will share how I solved it, but the path you take to get there is yours to choose.

Here is what we will build over the four weeks: An event-driven integration pipeline that consumes events from a broker, vectorizes the data within those event payloads, stores the vectors in a vector store, and makes them available for Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG).

The screenshot below can give you an idea of what a RAG pipeline can look like. If you want to learn more about how the integration flow below was built - check this out.

This is a challenge that sits at the intersection of two worlds that are increasingly converging: event-driven architectures and Generative AI. Understanding how to feed real-time, event-driven data into an AI pipeline is becoming a foundational skill — and that's exactly what we're going to build.

Links to each week will be added here as they are published:

  • Week 1: Getting familiar with the events (4th of May)
  • Week 2: Connecting to the broker and consuming events  (11th of May)
  • Week 3: Vectorizing the event payload (To be published  18th of May)
  • Week 4: Storing vectors and enabling RAG (To be published  25th of May)

entry.title: May 2026 Developer Challenge — Week 1: Getting familiar with the events
entry.content:
Welcome to the first week of the May 2026 Developer Challenge! 🎉

This month we are going to build something that bridges two worlds that are becoming increasingly intertwined: event-driven architectures and AI. By the end of the four weeks, you will have built an end-to-end pipeline that takes events from a broker, extracts and vectorizes the data in their payloads, and stores those vectors so they can be used for Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG).

But before we can build any of that, we need to understand the data we'll be working with. This week, we focus on the events themselves — what they look like, what data they carry, and how to publish them to a broker.

ℹ️ Reminder: The goal of the challenge is to learn the concepts, architect a solution and implement it using whatever tools you have access to — SAP, open-source, trial, paid products, use your favourite programming language or whatever agent you fancy using.

The scenario

Throughout this challenge, I will work with Business Partner events. A Business Partner event is published whenever a business partner (a customer, supplier, or employee) is created or changed in a system. These are common, well-understood events in enterprise landscapes, and their payloads contain rich, structured data that is well-suited for vectorization.

The standard Business Partner event, available out of the box in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, is a notification type event which contains minimal information. For simplicity purposes I will be using a custom "data type" Business Partner event, which just contains more data than usual.

We will use the CloudEvents specification as the format for our events. If you haven't worked with CloudEvents before, don't worry — we'll cover what you need to know. CloudEvents is a specification for describing event data in a common way. Its goal is to simplify event declaration and delivery across services and platforms. SAP has adopted it across its products, but it is also widely adopted across the open-source ecosystem.

Here is an example of a custom Business Partner Created data type event following the CloudEvents specification:

{
  "specversion": "1.0",
  "type": "sap.s4.custom.BusinessPartner.Created",
  "source": "/sap/s4/erp/business-partner",
  "id": "c54267d5-6318-488c-854a-c051602cde75",
  "time": "2026-04-29T15:32:38.947Z",
  "datacontenttype": "application/json",
  "subject": "1003770",
  "data": {
    "BusinessPartner": "1003770",
    "BusinessPartnerUUID": "89aa681d-5faf-4366-a813-e2d58445aea5",
    "BusinessPartnerFullName": "Manuel Abreu",
    "BusinessPartnerCategory": "1",
    "BusinessPartnerGrouping": "BP02",
    "FirstName": "Manuel",
    "LastName": "Abreu",
    "IsNaturalPerson": "X",
    "CreationDate": "/Date(1518393600000)/",
    "CreatedByUser": "CC0000000002",
    "BusinessPartnerAddress": {
      "Country": "PT",
      "Region": "",
      "CityName": "Praia da Vitória",
      "PostalCode": "6539-865",
      "StreetName": "Praça de Valente, 401",
      "HouseNumber": "12",
      "AddressTimeZone": "WEST"
    }
  }
}

You'll notice this event is a data event — unlike a pure notification event (which would only carry the BusinessPartner ID), this one includes fields that describe the business partner itself. That's intentional: richer data in the payload means more signal for our vectorization step later in the challenge.

Your task this week

👉 Publish at least three Business Partner events to a message broker of your choice.

The events should:

  1. Follow the CloudEvents specification (pay attention to the required context attributes: specversion, type, source, id, time, datacontenttype, data)
  2. Include meaningful data in the data field — not just an ID. Think name, region, country, type, etc.
  3. Be published to a topic that follows a sensible naming convention. For example: sap/s4/custom/BusinessPartner/Created/PT/1003770

Broker options — use whatever you have access to:

  • SAP options: SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh (AEM), SAP Cloud Application Event Hub, Event Mesh capability in SAP Integration Suite (EMIS).
  • Open-source options: Solace PubSub+ (free tier), RabbitMQ, or any other you might have available.

You can publish the events programmatically (recommended) or manually via a broker's management UI or REST API.

Generate events

To help you generating events, I've published a simple web app (Business Event Generator 📡) that can connect to an AEM broker or EMIS. With it, you can publish some Business Partners Create data events with the same structure as the one above. In case you don't have access to an AEM or EMIS instance, you can just copy the Business Partner data available in the repo and use it programatically to generate your own events.

The data published is not real data. It was all generated using the awesome 🐍 Python Faker library 😃

Share your work

To complete this week's challenge, add a comment in this discussion with:

  1. A screenshot or code snippet showing your events being published to your broker
  2. A sample of one of the CloudEvents you published (mask any sensitive data)
  3. Which broker you used and why you chose it

SAP solution note — I'm sharing in the comments below how I solved this using SAP Integration Suite + Advanced Event Mesh.

Further reading

  • CloudEvents specification
  • CloudEvents at SAP
  • SAP Business Accelerator Hub — Business Partner Events

Some food for thought:

  1. Why might you prefer a data event over a notification event for a RAG use case?
  2. What fields would you include in the data payload to make it most useful for semantic search later?
  3. You might have noticed that there is some dynamic data included in the example topic above... In which cases that would be useful?
  4. How does your chosen broker handle message persistence? What happens to a message if no consumer is connected when it arrives?

entry.title: Re: May 2026 Developer Challenge — Week 1: Getting familiar with the events
entry.content:
I'm a bit biased and I'm using the Business Event Generator shared above to send events :-). You can see that it will generate events exactly as the one shown above. In my case it is connected to AEM but I think I might switch to EMIS for next week.
Event generator connected to AEMSample event payload

entry.title: Meet up - Sapphire Madrid 2026
entry.content:
¡Hola comunidad SAP en Madrid! ¿Qué les parece si nos juntamos la noche despues del segundo dia de SAP Sapphire Madrid 2026? La idea es reunirnos para hacer networking, compartir lo que se ha anunciado como parte de SAP Sapphire. Comida y bebida a cargo de cada uno.

El evento sera en Taproom Madrid dentro de Tierra Burrito Bar, C. de San Germán, 6, Tetuán, 28020 Madrid. Por favor confirmar la asistencia con la funcionalidad de RSVP de esta pagina.

------

Hola SAP Community in Madrid! Join us the evening after the second day of Sapphire 2026 in Madrid for an informal dinner and networking session. Great chance to meet SAP Community member and exchange what we've learnt as part of Sapphire. Each person covers their own food and drinks.

The location is Taproom Madrid, dentro de Tierra Burrito Bar, C. de San Germán, 6, Tetuán, 28020 Madrid. Please confirm that you will be attending by using the RSVP functionality on this web page.

  • 28th of May - SAP Labs Latin America: 🇲🇽ABAP AI: Capabilities in SAP Joule for Developers CodeJam (Monterrey, Mexico)

entry.title: Re: May 2026 Developer Challenge — Week 1: Getting familiar with the events
entry.content: Very nice solution! Looks like you've implemented the entire scenario already hehe. I look forward to see how it evolves in the next couple of weeks :-).

entry.title: Re: May 2026 Developer Challenge — Week 1: Getting familiar with the events
entry.content: Nice! Thanks for sharing

entry.title: Re: May 2026 Developer Challenge — Week 1: Getting familiar with the events
entry.content: That's correct... AEM is powered by Solace technology so getting familiar and using Solace PubSub+ is a great way for learning AEM 🙂

entry.title: May 2026 Developer Challenge — Week 2: Connecting to the broker and consuming events
entry.content:
Welcome to week 2! Last week you published Business Partner events to a broker. This week, we shift to the **consumer side** — we will connect to that broker from an integration platform/runtime and consume those events.

This is where the "integration" in event-driven integration starts to take shape. Rather than a point-to-point call from producer to consumer, we're decoupling them via the broker. Your consumer doesn't need to know anything about where the events came from — it just subscribes to the topic and processes what arrives.
 

Your task this week

👉 Connect to your broker from an integration platform or runtime and consume the Business Partner events you published last week.

When an event is received, your integration should:

  1. Parse the CloudEvent payload
  2. Extract the data field
  3. Log or store the received payload (a simple log output or a file write is fine for now — we'll do something more interesting with it next week)

We haven't mentioned anything about storing the events or what would hapeen if the consumer is not available. Given how important business events normally are, you should consider using a storing mechanism, e.g. a queue, in case the consumer is unavailable and you don't want to miss any events.

Integration platform / runtime options:

  • SAP options: Cloud Integration (SAP Integration Suite), SAP Build Process Automation
  • Open-source / general options: Apache Camel, Node.js with your broker's SDK, Python with your broker's SDK, n8n

Your consumer does not need to be on the same technology stack as your publisher. Part of the beauty of a message broker is that producers and consumers are completely decoupled — they can be written in different languages, deployed on different infrastructure, and replaced independently.

Share your work

Add a comment in this discussion with:

  1. A screenshot or log output showing your integration receiving and parsing an event
  2. A brief description of what integration platform/runtime you used and how you connected to the broker (protocol used, e.g. AMQP, MQTT, REST)
  3. Any challenges you ran into connecting the two sides

SAP solution note — I'm sharing in the comments below how I solved this using SAP Integration Suite + Advanced Event Mesh. Also, I'm aware that some of you actually already completed this exercise as part of week 1 and that's completely fine. You can relax this week  🍹🏝

Some food for thought:

  1. What protocol did your broker support for consuming messages? Did you have a choice?
  2. What would happen in your setup if the consumer was offline when events were published? Would it receive them when it came back online? (This depends if you are storing those events, e.g. configuring a queue in the broker)
  3. How would you handle a malformed CloudEvent that arrives on the topic?

entry.title: Re: May 2026 Developer Challenge — Week 2: Connecting to the broker and consuming events
entry.content:
In my case, I first created a queue in AEM so I don't lose any valuable business events.

 

Then, I wanted to get familiar with the generate iFlow functionality in SAP Integration Suite. You can see my prompt below which generated an integration flow that will consume events from a queue in AEM.  You'll notice my mistake in the prompt, I specified Event Mesh which created an AMQP connection for the sender. I believe that if I would have specified AEM it will automatically used the AEM adapter.

 The generated iFlow plus my logging of events. This iFlow is using the AEM adapter, which uses the SMF protocol under the hood. 

The groovy script above extract the payload from the data field in the message.
